coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of FRET based nano sensor from biomass-derived fluorescent carbon quantum dots and naphthalimide for ratiometric detection of nitric oxide: To examine nitrite levels in meat samples
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Amanpreet
Singh, Gagandeep
Kaur, Navneet
Singh, Narinder
coredata.dc:description: Nitric oxide (NO) is a ubiquitous, gaseous, free radical signaling molecule which plays a key role in physiological and pathological processes. Literature reports revealed that the conventional methods such as colorimetry, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), electrochemical etc. to detect NO are costly, time consuming and lack resolution, particularly in aqueous or biological system. Thus, in this context, herein we have developed covalently linked biomass derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and naphthalimide based nano sensor system for FRET based ratiometric detection of nitric oxide (NO) in pure aqueous media. The CQDs derived from orange peels were characterized using UV–visible absorption, fluorescence spectroscopy, PXRD, TEM, FT-IR and zeta potential studies. Further, the obtained CQDs were functionalized with amine functionality, and subsequently linked with naphthalimide derivative (5) using terephthaldehyde through covalent bond formation. The conjugation of naphthalimide (5) and functionalized CQDs was studied using DLS, zeta potential, FT-IR and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. The excitation of developed nano sensor system at λ ex 360 nm results in fluorescence emission at λ em 530 nm which establishes the FRET pair between the CQDs and naphthalimide unit. However, in the presence of NO, the observed FRET pair abolishes due to the cleavage of NO susceptible imine bond. The developed sensor demonstrates high selectivity towards NO with limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of 15 nM and 50 nM respectively. Further, the developed sensor system was also utilized for indirect detection of nitrite (NO2 −) in food samples for food safety and monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
FRET
Nitric oxide
Fluorescence spectroscopy
Nitrite detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of functionalized CQDs and fabrication of nanosensor for NO detection
Characterization of CQDs and functionalized CQDs
Characterization of conjugation of f-CQDs with compound 5
Photophysical studies of nanosensor with various analytes
Effect of pH and salt concentration on the sensor assembly
Real time application for food safety and monitoring

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) UV–visible absorption spectrum of CQDs; B) Excitation dependent emission profile of CQDs; C) TEM image of prepared CQDs; D) PXRD spectrum of synthesized CQDs.
A) FT-IR spectrum of synthesized CQDs and functionalized CQDs containing acyl hydrazide groups; B) Zeta potential of CQDs (−23 mV) and functionalized CQDs (−6.5 mV).

A) UV–Visible absorption spectrum of CQDs, compound 5 and hybrid (sensor) respectively; B) Overlap of absorption spectrum of compound 5 with emission spectrum of f-CQDs; C) FT-IR spectrum of compound 5 and nanosensor; D) Zeta potential of f-CQDs and nanosensor.

A) Fluorescence titration of nano sensor (10 μM) with increasing concentration of nitric oxide (NO, 0–20 μM); B) Linear regression graph for titration of sensor with increasing concentration of NO (R2 value of 0.998); C) Graphical representation of NO sensing mechanism by FRET pair of naphthalimide and f-CQDs.
Graphical representation of NO sensor with plausible mechanism.
Preparation of functionalized CQDs and nanosensor.
Decay parameters obtained from time resolved fluorescence spectrometer and quantum yield.
Real sample analysis of nitrite in meat sample using developed FRET-based nano sensor.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of FRET based nano sensor from biomass-derived fluorescent carbon quantum dots and naphthalimide for ratiometric detection of nitric oxide: To examine nitrite levels in meat samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Punjab, 140001, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Punjab-140001, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Punjab, 140001, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Punjab-140001, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh-160014, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address; Department of Chemistry, University Institute of Science, Chandigarh University, Mohali, Punjab −140413, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitric oxide (NO) is a ubiquitous, gaseous, free radical signaling molecule which plays a key role in physiological and pathological processes. Literature reports revealed that the conventional methods such as colorimetry, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), electrochemical etc. to detect NO are costly, time consuming and lack resolution, particularly in aqueous or biological system. Thus, in this context, herein we have developed covalently linked biomass derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and naphthalimide based nano sensor system for FRET based ratiometric detection of nitric oxide (NO) in pure aqueous media. The CQDs derived from orange peels were characterized using UV–visible absorption, fluorescence spectroscopy, PXRD, TEM, FT-IR and zeta potential studies. Further, the obtained CQDs were functionalized with amine functionality, and subsequently linked with naphthalimide derivative (5) using terephthaldehyde through covalent bond formation. The conjugation of naphthalimide (5) and functionalized CQDs was studied using DLS, zeta potential, FT-IR and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. The excitation of developed nano sensor system at λ ex 360 nm results in fluorescence emission at λ em 530 nm which establishes the FRET pair between the CQDs and naphthalimide unit. However, in the presence of NO, the observed FRET pair abolishes due to the cleavage of NO susceptible imine bond. The developed sensor demonstrates high selectivity towards NO with limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of 15 nM and 50 nM respectively. Further, the developed sensor system was also utilized for indirect detection of nitrite (NO2 −) in food samples for food safety and monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The required data is provided in supporting information file.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 −) has significant effect on human health and ecological system. Perusal of literature reports revealed that various nitrite and nitrate salts have been used for preservation of meat and fish products [1]. These nitrites can transform the secondary amines into nitrosamines that are carcinogenic [2]. Additionally, nitrite restricts the binding of hemoglobin with oxygen [3]. In the biological system nitrites undergo reduction to nitric oxide (NO), via various enzymatic and non-enzymatic pathways [4]. Therefore, it has been recognized as storage pools for nitric oxide (NO). NO is emerging as an attractive molecule in chemistry and biochemistry due to its significant role in both physiological and biological processes [5]. It acts as a signaling molecule in the nervous, cardiovascular, and immune systems [6]. Moreover, studies indicated that irregular homeostasis of NO is associated with diabetes, stroke, tumor growth, hypertension, atherosclerosis, erectile dysfunction, and various neurodegenerative disorders [7,8]. Chemically, on account of the paramagnetic nature of NO, it is highly reactive and reacts with various radical species in tissues. In addition to chemical reactivity, lipid solubility and diffusibility in cells create an obstacle in detecting and capturing NO [9]. Although the role of nitrites and NO in biological system and environment is widely explored, the strategies reported so far for efficient, rapid, and sensitive detection of these analytes have several limitations [10]. For instance, various conventional methods employed for NO and nitrite detection are colorimetry, electron paramagnetic resonance (for NO), electrochemical and chemiluminescence etc. [11,12] However, these approaches are time-consuming, costly, and lack resolution, especially in biological systems. To overcome these issues related with economic detection of these two analytes, extensive research is ongoing on fluorescence-based techniques owing to their simplicity and high sensitivity, which provides their real-time detection [1,13]. Additionally, some of the fluorescence-based strategies employed in cell biology are noninvasive in nature and cause no harm to biological specimens [14]. These fluorescent receptors to detect NO includes transition metal complexes (of Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Rh2+, etc.) [15–18], o-phenylenediamine-based receptors [19], boron-based diamino probes [20], cyanine dyes [21], diamino fluorescein, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), inorganic quantum dots [22] etc. It is well established that the fluorescent probes possessing vicinal diamino groups cyclize into less electron-donating triazole rings during NO detection, which results into the loss of its quenching ability. Thus, the principle behind these diamino-based receptors formed the basis of many subsequent probes for detecting NO [23]. However, interference due to ascorbic acid, dehydroascorbic acid cannot be ignored. To overcome the shortcomings of receptors containing vicinal diamines, quinoline, and coumarin-based probes consisting acyl hydrazide moiety for NO has been reported in the literature which detects NO via the formation of oxazole ring [9]. Nevertheless, results obtained from these single fluorescence-based probes are affected by external factors such as concentration, excitation source, temperature, the solvent system used, and non-uniform mixing of organic receptor [24]. Apparently, these factors can influence the reproducibility of results for NO detection. Thus, to construct a reliable and suitable system for NO detection fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is an admirable choice to evade variation of results attributable to external factors [25]. Extensive studies have been performed on the basis of FRET-based approaches for bioanalysis, in order to construct a ratiometric fluorescent system, as the ratiometric sensors uses the ratio of two fluorescence intensities to qualitatively detect the analytes in comparison to those with one signal sensors [26]. In this way they can eliminate most ambiguities in the detection by self-calibrating two emission bands [27]. It is well known that FRET based sensing systems have been designed in the form of small molecular dyads consisting two fluorophores connected by spacer through covalent bond [28,29].However, such fluorescent dyads are difficult to prepare and still suffer from photobleaching and interference [30]. In the literature, various types of fluorescent nanoparticles (NPs) have been reported such as Au NPs, quantum dots (QDs), rare earth up-conversion nanospheres, carbon nanodots, and dye-labelled microspheres etc. [26] In particular, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) compared to organic dyes offer resistance to photobleaching activity and high quantum yield [31]. Also, they possess a high ratio of radiative to non-radiative transitions. Due to their biocompatibility [32], photostability [33], membrane permeability, water solubility [34], and tunable functionalization on surface [35], they are widely used for various sensing applications [36,37].

Therefore, in this context, herein, a ratiometric sensor system for NO and nitrite has been developed, and further, it can be utilized for the quantification of nitrite detection for food safety and monitoring, which comprises of biomass-derived CQDs coupled with naphthalimide through terephthaldehyde. The selection of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be rationalized in terms of their excellent fluorescence properties. These CQDs can act as better energy transfer units for the development of ratiometric sensor as compared to organic dyes. The most fascinating property of CQDs is excitation-dependent emission, due to which they can be adjusted to any wavelength for energy transfer, whereas the naphthalimide unit may act as an energy acceptor unit.

All the reagents, used to prepare functionalized CQDs and naphthalimide derivatives were purchased from various suppliers. Terephthaldehyde, 1,8-naphthalic anhydride, hydrazine hydrate, bromine, triphenylphosphine, sodium azide, and HCl were purchased from TCI chemicals. These chemicals were used as such without further purification. Solvents such as ethanol, THF, and DMSO were purchased from Avra synthesis. During the synthesis and analytical experiments, Milli-Q water was used. The UV–Visible absorption studies were performed on the Shimadzu instrument with cuvettes having 1 cm path length and water as a solvent. Fluorescence emission spectra were studied on the Perkin Elmer LS55 Fluorescence spectrophotometer instrument at room temperature with a slit width of 10 nm. CQDs were prepared in Muffle Furnace. The surface morphology of CQDs was studied on TEM instrument FEI (Tecnai G2 F-20) with a point-to-point resolution of 0.27 nm and working at 200 kV. DLS studies were performed on Metrohm Microtrac Ultra Nanotrac particle size analyzer using water as solvent. To characterize synthesized derivatives 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectrum were recorded on Jeol instrument. FT-IR spectra of all samples were performed on Brucker Tensor 27 spectrophotometer and Perkin Elmer (L1600300 Spectrum TWO LiTa). PXRD pattern of CQDs was studied using X’PERT PRO analytical instrument. Zeta potential measurements were performed on a Zeta-check particle charge reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of functionalized CQDs and fabrication of nanosensor for NO detection
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared from orange peels using the pyrolysis method reported in the literature [38]. Orange peels were dried and then pyrolyzed in a muffle furnace at 180 °C for 1 h. After pyrolysis, brown-colored material was so obtained washed with ethanol and followed by dialysis against water to obtain pure CQDs containing COOH functional groups. Then the obtained CQDs were refluxed in thionyl chloride results in the formation of CQDs containing -COCl functional groups on their surface. The excess thionyl chloride was distilled off, followed by adding a hydrazine hydrate solution to afford CQDs containing acyl hydrazide moiety. The prepared functionalized CQDs were stored at −4 °C for further use (Scheme 1 A). After the successful synthesis of functionalized CQDs; the compound 5 was synthesized as illustrated in scheme S1. Further, we have developed FRET pair between these two components using terephthaldehyde as a spacer unit. In order to synthesize the FRET pair 20 mg of functionalized CQDs, 20 mg of terephthaldehyde, and 20 mg of compound 5 were stirred for 4 h in one pot fashion. The color of the solution becomes reddish-brown on the completion of the reaction. After that, the product was dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h followed by centrifugation. The solid material obtained dried and stored at −4 °C for further use (Scheme 1B).

To study sensing of NO using synthesized FRET pair we have generated NO using the procedure reported in the literature [20]. Briefly, the methodology involves the reaction of a saturated solution of NaNO2 (50 g) with sulphuric acid (6 M, 125 ml) under the atmosphere of argon. In an acidic medium, sodium nitrate undergoes a disproportion reaction to produce nitric oxide. In order to remove other oxides of nitrogen, oxygen, and acid content, it is allowed to pass through pyrogallol solution and KOH solution, respectively. During the generation of NO, the assembly as well as solutions are degassed to exclude oxygen content. Further, in order to find out the concentration of NO solution formed, the Griess reaction was employed. The concentration of stock solution for NO used was 1 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained biomass derived CQDs displayed a broad absorption in the UV–visible absorption spectrum at 350 nm which is due to n- π* transition and it is consistent with literature reports (Fig. 1 A) [39]. The emission profile of these prepared CQDs has also been recorded in order to evaluate the tunable emission behavior by varying the excitation wavelength from λ ex 370 to λ ex 510 nm (Fig. 1B), wherein the decrease in fluorescence intensity along with red shift in emission maxima was observed as per literature [40].

Further, the morphological analysis by TEM demonstrated that biomass-derived CQDs had a spherical shape and were uniformly dispersed in the solution with average particle size of 7 nm whereas the hydrodynamic radius (Rh) of the prepared CQDs was found to be 10 nm which is slightly higher than the size analyzed from TEM images (Fig. 1C) [34]. The broad diffraction peak centered at 24° in PXRD indicated the amorphous nature of prepared CQDs (Fig. 1D) [31]. Thus, all the structural characterizations are consistent with the literature reports. In FT-IR spectrum of CQDs stretching band at 1707 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of COOH functional groups on the surface of CQDs. The broadband in the spectrum in the range of 2500 cm−1 to 3300 cm−1 is due to OH stretching. Also, there are two bands in the FT-IR spectrum at 3338 cm−1 and 3190 cm−1, which corresponds to the amino functional group in functionalized CQDs (Fig. 2 A).

In order to confirm the functionalization of CQDs, the FT-IR spectrum of functionalized CQDs was recorded. After the functionalization, the characteristic band of COOH group shifted from 1707 cm−1 to 1605 cm−1, the characteristic peak of acyl hydrazides reported in the literature (Fig. 2A) [41]. Consequently, upon functionalization with acyl hydrazide onto the surface of CQDs, the zeta potential of developed f-CQDs increases from −23mV to −6.5 mV which further confirms the functionalization of CQDs (Fig. 2B) [36].

Then the energy acceptor i.e., compound 5 was covalently attached with functionalized CQDs (f-CQDs) through imine bond formation with terephthaldehyde (Scheme 1B). The compound 5 was synthesized as illustrated in scheme S1. Initially, 1,8-naphthaldehyde (1) was brominated and reacted with hydrazine which led to the formation of compound 3 in good yields. The subsequent reaction of 3 with sodium azide in DMSO led to the formation of compound 4. Finally, the obtained compound 4 was subjected to Staudinger reaction which results in the formation of product 5 (Scheme S1). All the compounds were characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR spectroscopy, and mass spectrometry.

After successfully synthesizing functionalized CQDs and compound 5, we developed a FRET pair between these two components using terephthaldehyde as a spacer unit. In order to develop the FRET pair, 20 mg of functionalized CQDs, 20 mg of terephthaldehyde, and 20 mg of compound 5 were stirred for 4 h in one pot fashion. The color of the solution becomes reddish-brown on the completion of the reaction. After that, the product was dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h followed by centrifugation. The solid material obtained was dried and stored at −4 °C for further use (Scheme 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of conjugation of f-CQDs with compound 5
ce:section.ce:para: f-CQDs showed absorption band at λ 360 nm in UV–visible absorption spectrum while the compound 5 showed absorption band at 437 nm. On fabrication of nano sensor, there are two bands corresponding to CQDs and compound 5 with slight shift in absorption wavelength (Fig. 3 A). Further, the fluorescence emission spectrum of f-CQDs exhibits band at λ 460 nm when excited at λ 360 nm. Fig. 3B clearly revealed that absorption spectrum of compound 5 overlaps with the emission spectrum of f-CQDs, which can result in the formation of FRET pair between these two moieties. The FT-IR spectrum of nano sensor also reveals the band at 1670 cm−1, the characteristic band of CN stretching vibration. This indicates that functionalized CQDs and naphthalimide-based compound 5 is conjugated with terephthaldehyde through imine bond formation (Fig. 3C). Further, there is a change in Zeta potential on the conjugation of f-CQDs with naphthalimide (Fig. 3D). The zeta potential of nanosensor gives the value of 12 mv whereas functionalized CQDs show a zeta potential of −6.5 mV, indicating that functionalized CQDs get conjugated with naphthalimide. Further, the hydrodynamic size of developed CQDs conjugated with naphthalimide has been investigated by DLS. On conjugation, the increase in hydrodynamic radii was observed (Figs. S7 and S8) as compared to pristine CQDs. Further, the increase in hydrodynamic radii after conjugation of f-CQDs with compound 5 has also been revealed by HRTEM analysis (Fig. S15). In order to examine energy transfer from CQDs to naphthalimide, time-resolved fluorescence has been performed wherein the decay profile of CQDs shows Γ1 and Γ2 value 13 ns and 2.12 ns, respectively; whereas, for developed nanosensor the values of Γ1 and Γ2 obtained from the decay experiment are 6 ns and 1.2 ns, respectively, which revealed that decrease in the values of decay time corresponds to the energy transferred from CQDs to naphthalimide (Table 1 and Fig. S9-S10) [42,43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical studies of nanosensor with various analytes
ce:section.ce:para: Earlier, Singh et al. have reported numerous naphthalimide-based probes for detecting various elevated enzymes in response to respective disorders [29,36,44]. It is well established that to construct a FRET mechanism between two fluorophores, the fluorescence emission spectra of one fluorophore must be overlapped with absorption spectrum of another fluorophore unit as evident from Fig. 3B [25]. We envisaged that the designed probe may act through FRET-based mechanism in between naphthalimide (5, acceptor unit) and CQDs (donor unit) for sensing NO. Consequently, the photophysical studies revealed that the excitation of developed sensor system at λ 360 nm results in fluorescence emission band at λ 530 nm which confirms the transfer of energy from CQDs to naphthalimide unit through FRET mechanism. To study the binding of NO with the sensor system, the solution of the sensor (10 μM) was taken in the cuvette. To this solution successive addition of NO solution (0–20 μL) was added which led to the increase in fluorescence intensity at λ 460 nm along with decrease in intensity of the band at λ 530 nm (Fig. 4 A). The linear regression graph for binding of NO with developed sensor gives R2 value of 0.998 (Fig. 4B). The literature reports revealed that the imine linkage is susceptible to nitric oxide, and thus, in the presence of NO this imine linkage results in the cleavage of FRET pair as shown in (Fig. 4C) [14]. Further, the selectivity of sensor towards NO over other analytes such as N3 −, ClO−, NO2 -, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2− S2O3 2−, SO3 2−, ascorbic acid, glutathione (GSH), cysteine, methionine, H2O2 etc. was investigated by addition of 20 μL of each analyte (1 mM) solution. It was observed that the sensor system shows ratiometric response on addition of NO, whereas on addition of other analytes, there is no change in fluorescence profile of nano sensor as depicted in Fig. S11. Further, competitive binding studies of sensor containing NO solution were performed by addition of 20 μL of other analytes. It is revealed from Fig. S12, that there is no change in fluorescence intensity of nano sensor containing NO, on addition of 20 μL of other analytes (1 mM). Further, the limit of detection (15 nM) of the prepared sensor was calculated using the 3σ method, whereas, the limit of quantification (50 nM) was calculated using the 10 σ method [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and salt concentration on the sensor assembly
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the sensor assembly was studied and it was observed that in the pH range of 5–10, there is no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of sensor. This indicates that the developed sensor can be utilized in the pH range of 5–9 (Fig. S13). Additionally, the effect of salt concentration on the sensor and sensor containing NO fluorescence profile was studied (Figs. S14 and S15). The time dependent fluorescence studies on addition of NO to sensor has also been investigated and quick response was observed on addition of different concentrations of nitric oxide (4 μM, 8 μM, 12 μM; Fig. S12).

Due to the cleavage of the FRET pair, CQDs became free. The acyl hydrazide functional group is present on the surface of CQDs undergoes cyclization reaction with NO to form an oxazole ring. Consequently, the FRET becomes off results in the emission at λ 460 nm. In the fluorescence emission profile, the band's intensity at λ 530 nm decreased while the band's intensity at λ 460 nm increases on addition of NO (Fig. 5 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real time application for food safety and monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: Perusal of literature reports revealed that various nitrite salts have been used as an additive in food products for flavor, color and to prevent the growth of microorganisms. However, the excess of these nitrites can produce nitrosamines on reacting with secondary amines, which are carcinogenic in nature. It is well established that the presence of nitrite in the biological system may restrict the oxygen-carrying capacity of hemoglobin [2]. Apparently, various methods have been reported for the detection of nitrite which include gas chromatography, HPLC, electrochemical and spectroscopic techniques [45]. While, most of the methods involve the indirect detection and quantification of nitrite through the detection of nitric oxide which is the reduced product of nitrite. Therefore, inspired from this development, the developed NO sensor has also been used for indirect detection and quantification of nitrite in meat samples for food safety and monitoring in order to demonstrate the real-time application of the developed sensor system. The samples were prepared according to the protocol reported in the literature [2]. The prepared samples were spiked with three different concentrations of sodium nitrite. The samples were analyzed using a developed NO sensor, and the results obtained are shown in Table 2 .

In conclusion, we have fabricated FRET pair from biomass-derived fluorescent carbon quantum dots and naphthalimide (5) for the ratiometric detection of nitric oxide in an aqueous medium. The conjugation of CQDs and naphthalimide (5) is characterized using various spectroscopic techniques and analyzed for its response towards NO using fluorescence spectroscopy. Further, the time-dependent fluorescence studies revealed the quick response of the sensor towards NO. The prepared sensor can be used in wide pH range of 5–9 without any significant change in the emission profile. Consequently, real sample analysis confirms the practical utility of developed sensor for indirect nitrite sensing for food safety and monitoring.

Amanpreet Singh: Experiments, Data curation, Formal analysis. Gagandeep Singh: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Navneet Kaur: Conceptualization, Supervision. Narinder Singh: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to IIT Ropar for research facilities, and acknowledges financial assistance in the form of sponsored research projects (SYST-SP/YO/2019/1609 and DST/SEED/SCSP/STI/2019/108) by SEED division of Department of Science & Technology, Government of India.
